Tire out a group of enemies with your signet of weariness, afflicting them with {@condition weakness|GW} and lowering their initiative.

Choose a point that you can see within range. Each creature in a 15-foot-radius sphere centered on that point must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a target is {@condition weakness|GW|weakened} for the duration, and their initiative gets reduced by {@dice 1d8}. On a successful save, the target's initiative is reduced by half as much and is affected for 1 round only.
At the end of each of its turns, a target can make a Constitution saving throw. On a success, the condition ends.
Signet of Weariness
Signet. Target foe and all nearby foes lose 3...7...8 Energy and suffer from Weakness for 1...10...12 second[s].

spell.name: Searing Heat
entries: Cause an area of extreme heat, searing all those inside.

Choose a point that you can see within range. For the duration, you create a 15-foot-radius Sphere centered on that point. Each creature in that Sphere must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, a target takes {@damage 6d6} fire damage. On a successful save, a target takes half as much damage. A creature also makes this save if it enters the Sphere or ends its turn there, or if the Sphere moves into its space. A creature makes this save only once per turn.
On your later turns, you can take a {@action Magic|XPHB} action to move the Sphere in a straight line up to 60 feet.
When this spell ends, all creatures in the spell's area start {@condition burning|GW} for 1 minute, taking {@damage 2d6} damage.
At the end of each of its turns, a target can make a Dexterity saving throw. On a success, the condition ends.

components.m: a handful of coals and a bellow

entriesHigherLevel.name: Using a Higher-Level Spell Slot
entries: The damage increases by {@dice 1d6} for each slot level above 3.
Searing Heat

Spell. Cause Searing Heat at target foe's location. For 5 seconds, foes near this location are struck for 10...34...40 fire damage each second. When Searing Heat ends, foes in the area of effect are set on fire for 3 seconds.

spell.name: Scourge Sacrifice
entries: {@variantrule Hex|GW}
Muddle the power of blood for a group of creatures, requiring them to sacrifice twice as much health for sacrifice spells.

Choose a point that you can see within range. Each creature in a 10-foot-radius sphere centered on that point must make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, for the duration, when a target sacrifices health for a spell, they must sacrifice 100% more. On a successful save, a target is affected for 1 round only.
Scourge Sacrifice
Hex Spell. For 8...18...20 seconds, every time target foe and adjacent foes sacrifice life, they sacrifice twice the normal amount.

entriesHigherLevel.name: At Higher Levels
entries: The radius of the spell increases by 5 feet for each slot level above 1.

spell.name: Verata's Sacrifice
entries: {@variantrule Sacrifice|GW|Sacrifice:} 15%
Channel the devotion to his minions the great Necromancer Verata had, sacrificing yourself to heal them and cleanse them of most conditions.

Choose any number of friendly undead creatures, which have been created by a spell or an ability, that you can see within range. You gain a copy of each harmful condition on a target. End all copied conditions on a target. Additionally, for the duration at the start of each of a target's turns, a target regains hit points equal to {@dice 4d4}.
If a target drops to 0 hit points, this spell ends on them.
If you cast this spell while you have control over less than 4 friendly undead creatures, which have been created by a spell or an ability, you regain the spell slot expended in the casting.
Verata's Sacrifice

Spell. For 5...9...10 seconds, your undead allies gain +10 Health regeneration. All conditions are removed from those allies and transferred to you. If this spell is successful and you have control of 3 or fewer minions, Verata's Sacrifice instantly recharges.

components.m: a drop of blood

entriesHigherLevel.name: Using a Higher-Level Spell Slot
entries: The healing each increases  by {@dice 2d4} for each slot level above 3.

spell.name: Symbiotic Bond
entries: {@variantrule Companion|GW}, {@variantrule Shout|GW}
Form a symbiotic bond between yourself and a companion, giving them health regeneration and absorbing damage for them.

Choose one companion that can hear you within range. For the duration, the companion has resistance to all damage. Also, each time the companion takes damage, you lose health equal to the damage taken. Additionally, for the duration, at the start of each of the companion's turns, the companion regains hit points equal to {@dice 1d4}.
If the target drops to 0 hit points, this spell ends.
Symbiotic Bond
Shout. For 120...264...300 seconds, your animal companion gains 1...3...3 Health regeneration, and half of all damage dealt to your animal companion is redirected to you.

entriesHigherLevel.name: Using a Higher-Level Spell Slot
entries: The healing increases by {@dice 1d4} for each slot level above 3. The duration increases to 8 hours (level 4-5 slot) or 24 hours (level 6+ slot).

spell.name: Spinal Shivers
entries: {@variantrule Hex|GW}
Make a creature extremely sensitive to the cold, interrupting them every time they take cold damage.

Choose one creature that you can see within range. For the duration, when the target takes cold damage, the target must make a Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the target can't cast spells or use abilities until the end of their next turn.
Spinal Shivers
Hex Spell. For 10...34...40 seconds, whenever target foe is struck for cold damage while using a skill, that foe is interrupted, and you lose 8...4...3 Energy or Spinal Shivers ends.

entriesHigherLevel.name: At Higher Levels
entries: You can target one additional creature for each slot level above 3.

spell.name: Bestial Pounce
entries: {@variantrule Companion|GW}
Command your pet to attempt a Bestial Pounce, knocking down creatures who cast a spell on their last turn.

Choose one companion that can hear you within range. The companion uses its reaction to make an attack against a creature of your choice that the companion can see within the companion's range. On a hit, the target suffers the attack's normal effects and takes {@damage 2d10} damage, and, if the target cast a spell on its last turn, the target must make a Strength saving throw. On a failed save, the target is knocked {@condition prone}.
Bestial Pounce
Pet Attack. Your animal companion attempts a Bestial Pounce that deals +5...17...20 damage. If the attack strikes a foe who is casting a spell, that foe is knocked down.

entriesHigherLevel.name: At Higher Levels
entries: The number of attacks increases by 1 for each slot level above 1.

table.name: The Tournament of Beasts Schedule
table.caption: Every Fithday, Everything strictly non-leathal, with healers on stand-by.
rows: One participant, "The Prey", gets a headstart, and gets chased by the rest of the participants around a circular track, filled with obstacles. The first participant to touch them wins, becoming "The Prey" for the next time. Can happen multiple times, depending on the length of each run.
No magic and no special gear.*
For one hour participants run a circular track, trying to get as many laps as possible.
No magic and no special gear.*

A special melee in which the young of each species fight each other with wooden weaponry and heavily protected armor. Free for all, last one standing, divded by weight classes. Each one lasts for a maximum of 10 minutes.
No magic and no special gear, only children.*

Originally called the "The Melee" it quickly became know as the "The Big One" as it was by far the most popular. Regualar melee, dulled non-magical weapons, heavily protected armor, free for all, last one standing, divided by weight classes, lasting maxium 10 minutes each.
No magic and no special gear.*
One versus One fights of previous "The Big One" winners. Dulled weapons, heavily protected armor, divided by classes, lasting maxium 10 minutes each.
Only "The Big One" winners. No magic and no special gear.*
A large open air feast where everyone can get free food and drink.
A series of magical duels in tournament style brackets. Winner is crowned "Magic Incarnate", getting a 25% discount on all foci in the henge until they loose their title.
The ability to use magic, no special gear.*
A series of non-magical duels in tournament style brackets. Winner is crowned "Chapion of the Arena", getting a 25% discount on all weapons and armor in the henge until they loose their title.
No magic and no special gear.*

A series of {@item Dragonchess Set|XPHB|Dragonchess} games in tournament style brackets. Winner is crowned "Mastermind", getting a 25% discount on all wonderous items in the henge until they loose their title.
No magic and no special gear.*
footnotes: * Magic and Special gear allowed only the Tournament after a Full Moon.

table.name: Flight to the Glade
rows: While traveling along a narrow path on the wall of a large mesa a rockslide threatens the group. To quickly react and stop the rockslide from preventing further travel the characters must succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d12} group Initiative roll. On a failure they loose {@dice 1d12} hours as rocks bury the path and it must either be cleared or an aletrnative route must be found.

A tropical storm harries the group. To quickly find adequate shelter to weather the storm and not loose too much energy fighting it, the characters must succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d10} Wisdom ({@skill Perception} or {@skill Survival}) group check. On a failure they loose {@dice 1d10} hours as fighting through the storm drains the energy of the group.

The group enters the terretory of dangerous hostile wildlife. To savely pass they must succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d8} Wisdom ({@skill Animal Handling}) or Charisma ({@skill Intimidation}) group check. On a failure they loose {@dice 1d8} hours as they must fight off the wildlife or find a way around it.

The group encounters an extremly thick patch of jungle. To quickly make their way through it they must succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d6} Strength ({@skill Athletics}) group check. On a failure they loose {@dice 1d6} hours as they must slowly make their way through it.

The group travels through a magically warded area. To disarm the wards they must succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d4} Intelligence ({@skill Arcana}) group check. On a failure they loos {@dice 1d4} hours as they are trapped in magical stasis.

The group comes accross a large canyon with floating stones throughout it. With a successful DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d4} Intelligence ({@skill Arcana}) group check they can read the patterns and learn to stabalize them enough to cross. On a success they gain {@dice 1d4} hours as they are quickly able to cross the canyon.

A neutral centaur tribe observes the group from high atop a mesa. The characters easily notice them, and if they talk to them and succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d6} Strength ({@skill Athletics}) or Dexterity ({@skill Acrobatics}) group check, besting them in friendly competition, they can gain their support. On a success they gain {@dice 1d6} hours as the centaurs point out easier travel paths.

The group enters the terretory of peaceful friendly wildlife. With a successful DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d8} Wisdom ({@skill Animal Handling}) group check they can convince the wildlife to aid them. On a success they gain {@dice 1d8} hours as the animals do their best to aid them, carrying slow group members or making it harder for the White Mantle to pass.

The weather clears up allowing the group to travel quicker. To fully capitalize on the good weather the characters must succeed on a DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d10} Wisdom ({@skill Perception} or {@skill Survival}) group check. On a success they gain {@dice 1d10} hours as they discover paths previously to treacherous to travel.

A group of friendly jungle spirits comes across the group, giggeling and playing as they make their way through the jungle. With a successful DC 10 + PB + {@dice 1d12} Intelligence ({@skill Nature}), Wisdom ({@skill Survival}) or Charisma ({@skill Persuasion} or {@skill Performance}) group check the characters can convince the fickle spirits to lend them their aid. On a success they gain {@dice 1d12} hours as the spirits show them secret paths through the jungle, hinder their enemies, or magically enhance the groups speed.

table.name: Pre-Searing Terrain Encounters
rows: Velit reiciendis alias fugit eveniet odio quos error. Perferendis ut ab voluptatibus corporis. Illum est facilis omnis quisquam pariatur ullam. Magnam ea non perferendis.

table.name: Pre-Searing Creature Encounters
rows: Velit reiciendis alias fugit eveniet odio quos error. Perferendis ut ab voluptatibus corporis. Illum est facilis omnis quisquam pariatur ullam. Magnam ea non perferendis.

background.name: Ascalon
entries: Once, Ascalon was a beautiful, fertile land of rolling green countryside and magnificent cities. Her people were viewed as grim by their neighbors. This was perhaps, to be expected, given their never-ending war against the aggressive Charr. Indeed, it was their unfailing vigilance, their Great Northern Wall, and the blood they shed each year to defend it that had protected not only Ascalon, but also Kryta and Orr through the ages.
Then came the invasion, and with it, the Searing.

Anyone alive today will remember the day the lands of Ascalon were blasted and torn with magic fire. Whole cities and guilds were destroyed in the Searing, and the might of Ascalon was shattered. Now the Great Northern Wall lies broken, and the Charr have overrun much of the kingdom, defiling it with their unholy shrines, killing those who stand in their way.

The survival of Ascalon hinges on but a single remaining corner of the fallen kingdom—the capital city of Rin. In the final years of the last Guild War, the people of Rin looked to a soldier named Adelbern, a simple man of humble origins, who rallied the people with his courage and cunning and steeled them not only against the guilds of Orr and Kryta but also against the terrors of the Charr.

The sudden destruction of most of the kingdom during the Searing has taken much of the fight out of the man now known as King Adelbern. He has become stubborn and set in his ways, afraid of losing what little he has left. But in his son Rurik, the people see a leader with the courage to perhaps help them reclaim their fallen kingdom.

The survivors of Ascalon live in a state of constant warfare, using hit-and-run tactics and the remnants of the Great Wall to prevent any significant advances by the Charr into their territory. King Adelbern has circled the wagons, so to speak, content to simply defend what Ascalon has left and live to fight another day. Prince Rurik, on the other hand, is far more daring than his father thinks is wise, and has even suggested that the time may be coming to launch an offensive against the Charr.

Already the rumbling of the winds of change can be heard in the streets. People are frightened. They wonder what will become of them. Some even wonder aloud if Adelbern has lost what it takes to steer Ascalon back from the brink. They wish to see the prince step up and take command of the kingdom. Perhaps under his guidance, the people of Ascalon will live on to see another golden age.

background.name: Crystal Desert
entries: Legends tell of a time, thousands of years ago, when the Crystal Desert was covered in water. These legends claim that it was the gods who raised the land, leaving it bare and empty in order to give the solitary creatures of the world a place to call their own. If the legends are true, humans were not among those for whom this land was made. There have been attempts by humans to settle in the desert, but they have, without exception, failed miserably, leaving behind only the grand monuments they built here proclaiming their short-lived triumph over this harsh land.

The weather in the Crystal Desert is hot and unforgiving. The winds blow hard, making and unmaking dunes, covering up the present and uncovering the past. An examination of the sand will reveal that each grain is actually a tiny, pointed crystal. In isolated locations, larger crystal formations have been revealed by the constant, unforgiving wind.

background.name: Maguuma Jungle
entries: Years ago, many tribes of human druids lived among the Maguuma's lush greenery and wildlife. But it has been a long time since anyone has heard word from them, and many believe that they were eaten by hostile jungle creatures or simply swallowed whole by the man-eating plants now rampant in the jungle.

The higher elevations are mostly devoid of water. Only the largest and hardiest of plants can live here. But down farther, closer to the water table, the Maguuma grows thick. The vegetation can become so dense that there are areas of the jungle floor that have never been touched by the sun's light.

background.name: Northeastern Kryta
entries: There are two types of humans in Kryta: those who worship the mysterious Unseen Ones and those who do not. The worshippers have become known as the White Mantle because of the long white sleeveless robes many of them wear.

It is the responsibility of the White Mantle to oversee the other humans and impose upon them the rules and laws of the Unseen Ones. Those inside the organization receive special privileges (more food, better clothing, access to books) simply for abiding by the rules of the Unseen Ones and carrying out the orders handed down by the White Mantle high priest. To the Krytans the White Mantle are the root of law and order, the protectors or saviors, if you will, of their lands.

The White Mantle maintain a series of temples all over the continent. Members of this organization frequently, though not always, choose to reside in these temples to better perform their duties to the Mantle and to more effectively worship the Unseen Ones.

Since the end of the last Guild War and the repelling of the Charr invasion, the White Mantle have maintained a high level of military preparedness. They don’t ever want to be caught off guard again, and they often keep large stores of weapons inside their temples to use in case of emergency. It’s also not uncommon for followers of the Mantle philosophy to be highly trained warriors. Fighting skills will come in handy if the day comes when they will need to once again defend Kryta from invasion.

background.name: Northern Shiverpeaks
entries: The Northern Shiverpeaks are, despite the name, a central region within the Shiverpeak Mountains. Inhabited mainly by the Deldrimor and Stone Summit Dwarves, they are also occupied by Centaurs and Ettins. They are often used as the primary route to cross the Shiverpeaks, with the Frost Gate barring one of two passes. There are few to no primary Deldrimor settlements in the Northern Shiverpeaks, but the Stone Summit are known to have mines here.

background.name: Pre-Searing
entries: Once, Ascalon was a beautiful, fertile land of rolling green countryside and magnificent cities. Her people were viewed as a bit grim by their neighbors, but this was perhaps to be expected, given their never-ending war against the Charr. Indeed, it was their unfailing vigilance, their Great Northern Wall, and the blood they shed each year to defend it that had protected not only Ascalon, but also Kryta and Orr through the ages.

background.name: Ring of Fire Islands
entries: Just south of the Tarnished Coast lie the volcanic islands that together make up the Ring of Fire. The large volcano at the center of the ring is where the gods reportedly dropped the Bloodstones before leaving Tyria for good. Of the islands that surround this one, many are still active. Ships that pass by the Ring report hearing the tell-tale hiss of scalding hot lava hitting the ocean, instantly vaporizing the saltwater into steam.

The reefs off the islands are formed of black pumice stone. There are very few natural ports, and those that might be suitable for docking would bring the crew of the ship within danger of being caught by a sudden, unexpected eruption.
There are no known settlements in the Ring of Fire.

background.name: Southern Shiverpeaks
entries: The Southern Shiverpeaks is, as the name implies, southern portion of the Shiverpeak Mountains and home to the capital of the Dwarves. In contrast to the Northern Shiverpeaks, this segment of the mountain range is considerably harsher. All kinds of wildlife, such as Tundra Giants, Frost Wurms, Ice Imps, Blessed Griffons, and Mountain Trolls, roam the snowy tundra, and, in addition to Deldrimor and the Stone Summit, it is currently occupied by other warring factions: the Avicara Tengu, White Mantle, Shining Blade, and Mursaat. The northern and southern areas are connected by several long, winding, and treacherous valleys, heavily populated with dangerous indigenous creatures.

background.name: Southwestern Kryta
entries: There are two types of humans in Kryta: those who worship the mysterious Unseen Ones and those who do not. The worshippers have become known as the White Mantle because of the long white sleeveless robes many of them wear.

It is the responsibility of the White Mantle to oversee the other humans and impose upon them the rules and laws of the Unseen Ones. Those inside the organization receive special privileges (more food, better clothing, access to books) simply for abiding by the rules of the Unseen Ones and carrying out the orders handed down by the White Mantle high priest. To the Krytans the White Mantle are the root of law and order, the protectors or saviors, if you will, of their lands.

The White Mantle maintain a series of temples all over the continent. Members of this organization frequently, though not always, choose to reside in these temples to better perform their duties to the Mantle and to more effectively worship the Unseen Ones.

Since the end of the last Guild War and the repelling of the Charr invasion, the White Mantle have maintained a high level of military preparedness. They don’t ever want to be caught off guard again, and they often keep large stores of weapons inside their temples to use in case of emergency. It’s also not uncommon for followers of the Mantle philosophy to be highly trained warriors. Fighting skills will come in handy if the day comes when they will need to once again defend Kryta from invasion.

legendaryGroup.name: Bone Dragon
regionalEffects: The region containing an adult or ancient bone dragon's lair is warped by its presence, creating the following effects:

items.name: Bone Spikes
entries: Bone spikes cover the area within 1 mile of the lair, rendering that area {@variantrule Difficult Terrain|XPHB}. Travel for creatures other than the dragon and its allies takes twice the usual time in that area.

items.name: Corrupted Water
entries: Water sources within 1 mile of the lair are supernaturally corrupted. A creature that drinks such water must succeed on a {@dc 15} Constitution saving throw or have the {@condition Unconscious|XPHB} condition for 1 minute.

regionalEffects: If the dragon dies or moves its lair elsewhere, these effects end immediately.

legendaryGroup.name: Screamheart
lairActions: When fighting inside its lair, a Screamheart can invoke the ambient magic to take lair actions. At the start of the round after the first one, the Screamheart can take one lair action to cause the following effect:
Summon forth a {@creature Creeping Shadow|GWP} from a random sarcophagus within 120 feet of you.

legendaryGroup.name: Dark Oak
lairActions: When fighting inside their lair, Dark Oak can take lair action. On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), Dark Oak can take one lair action to cause one of the following effects; Dark Oak can't use the same lair action two rounds in a row:
{@b Root Smash.} Dark Oak chooses one creature it can see and makes one attack against them (+15 to hit). On a hit, the target takes {@damage 3d12} bludgeoning damage.

{@b Root Grab.} Dark Oak chooses one creature it can see and forces them to make a {@actSave str} {@dc 25}. On a failed save, the creature is {@condition Restrained|XPHB} for 1 minute. On a successful save, the target is {@condition Restrained|XPHB} for 1 round only.

entries.name: Resilience
entries: After you fail a {@variantrule D20 Test|XPHB}, you can gain a {@variantrule Heroic Inspiration|XPHB}, if you do not already have one. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB}, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.

entries.name: Versatility
entries: You gain {@variantrule Proficiency|XPHB} in two {@variantrule Skill|XPHB|Skills} or Tools of your choice.

entries.name: Human Lineage
entries: You part of a lineage that grants you special traits. Choose a lineage from the Human Lineages table. You gain the level 1 benefit of that lineage. When you reach levels 3 and 5, your gain additional benefits.

If you learn a spell through your lineage the following rules apply: You always have that spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast the spell using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Whenever you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB} you can replace that spell with a different spell of the same level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for the spells you cast with this trait (choose the ability when you select the lineage).

entries.name: Human Lineages
rows: Your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum increases by 1, and it increases by 1 again whenever you gain a level.
You gain a +1 bonus to your {@variantrule Armor Class|XPHB}.

When you roll {@variantrule Initiative|XPHB} you can expend a {@variantrule Heroic Inspiration|XPHB} to gain advantage on the roll and gain a bonus to it equal to your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB}.

Moving through nonmagical {@variantrule Difficult Terrain|XPHB} costs you no extra movement. You can also pass through nonmagical plants without being slowed by them and without taking damage from them if they have thorns, spines, or a similar hazard.

When you use your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} during your turn you can expend a {@variantrule Heroic Inspiration|XPHB} to gain a bonus to your {@variantrule Speed|XPHB} until the start of your next turn. The bonus equals ten times your {@table Proficiency Bonus|XPHB}.
You learn the {@spell Magic Missile|XPHB} spell.
You learn the {@spell Misty Step|XPHB} spell.

entries: Humans are one of the most widespread and influential species in the world of Tyria. They are spread throughout the world of Tyria, including Cantha, Elona, and the eponymous continent Tyria, though it is implied they live further south than Cantha, which is where they may have originated.
Wherever they originated, humans are not native to the world as they were brought there by the Six Gods. The gods themselves only predate humans by a short period of time.
Humans arrived on the Tyrian continent in 205 BE. Since their arrival, three major human polities have arisen, Ascalon, Kryta, and Orr. Orr was the first region colonised.

Before the Exodus of the Gods, the gods lived in Arah (nicknamed the City of the Gods) and aided humans in numerous tasks such as pushing the Charr north so that the settlements that would become known as Ascalon could be established. With their aid, King Doric became the first ruler of all human realms. It was he who prayed to Dwayna to stop a war in 115 BE; it was he who pleaded to the gods a year before the exodus to stop the many conflicts caused by the gift of magic being given too freely; and it is his blood that seals the bloodstones.

After the creation of the bloodstones, the colonies of Ascalon and Kryta gradually split off into their own kingdoms - turning the unified human kingdom into three. During this time, open conflicts raged between the various human guilds and are now referred to as the Guild Wars. The Guild Wars were so widespread that they drew the Tyrian human kingdoms into war with each other in order to stop the battling in their own streets. It took the foreign threat of Charr to finally bring an end to the internecine conflict. The event now known as the Charr invasion ravaged the three human kingdoms, forcing them to put aside their squabbling or face destruction.

entries.name: Ascalonian
entries: Ascalon is a land filled with wheat fields, blossoming flowers, and natural irrigation throughout the fertile landscape. Ascalon is located between the Shiverpeak Mountains and the Blazeridge Mountains.

During the last Guild War, the people of Rin looked to Adelbern to lead them; he was crowned king due to his feats and popularity. Duke Barradin was the next heir to the throne before Adelbern was crowned and, as such, some of his supporters were not happy when a soldier was named instead. Some royalists, including nobles and members of the court, became bandits and took to the countryside.
Even though Ascalon is a beautiful place, it is riddled with threats from Skale, Grawl, bandits, and the Charr.

entries.name: Krytan
entries: Kryta was the third and last human kingdom to be established in Tyria. Originally a colony of Elona, it was established in 300 AE, it gained its independence in 358 AE. The kingdom's royal family are descendants of King Doric, however it is not known how far Doric's lineage goes into the Krytan royalty. There have been few notable members of this royalty, outside the last king, King Jadon, the only known monarch is Mad King Thorn. Those of the royal bloodline are buried in the Mausoleum, which is guarded by the undead dragon Rotscale.

Kryta is home to many important landmarks, such as the Wizard's Tower and the Temple of the Ages. It is also the origin of the now-mysterious Druids, former Krytans who left into the Maguuma Jungle to the west long ago.
Sometime in the distant past, several large watchtowers were built along the northern coast to defend Kryta against a threat to the north.

entries.name: Orrian
entries: Situated on a peninsula south of Ascalon and west of the Crystal Desert, Orr was a vibrant, proud, and prosperous nation. Its citizens were the favoured of the gods, living in the shadows of Arah, the deserted city once inhabited by the likes of Melandru, Dwayna, and Balthazar. Deeply spiritual, the Orrians looked after the buildings and structures left behind when the gods left Tyria, caring for them in the hopes that one day, the divine beings who created magic and bestowed it upon the world would return.

The Orrians were a peaceful people, hoping only to do their duty toward their gods and content to be rewarded in either this life or the next. When the guilds began feuding, Orr as a nation tried to stay out of the conflict. This was not the sort of struggle that entire kingdoms got involved in. But when the strife overflowed into armed conflict, and guilds from the other human nations began fighting in the streets of Arah, Orr rose to defend itself and the city of the gods.

Soon after Orr mobilised its armies, Kryta and Ascalon did as well, and what had started as a dispute between localised groups became an all-out war. The Guild Wars raged for nearly fifty years. During that time, none of the three human empires was able to assert dominance over either of the other two. While Ascalon, Orr, and Kryta were busy fighting with each other, they became blind to the threat from the north—the Charr.

The Kingdom of Orr was the first human kingdom established on the continent of Tyria, created by both god and man. From 100 BE, the kingdom was ruled alongside Ascalon by King Doric until his death in 1BE. Orr gained its independence in the year 2 AE. At the time of the Second Great Corsair War and King Zoran's reign, Orrian territory is known to have extended into the Tarnished Coast, and included the caverns now known as the Shards of Orr. Orr was once the most vibrant, proud, and prosperous human nation in the known world. Its citizens believed themselves to be the favoured of the Six Human Gods, living in the shadows of Arah which was once inhabited by the gods themselves. Orr was known as the nexus of arcane activity and research.

entries.name: Darkvision
entries: You have {@sense Darkvision} with a range of 120 feet.

entries.name: Resilience
entries: You have {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to Poison damage. You also have {@variantrule Advantage|XPHB} on saving throws you make to avoid the {@condition Poisoned|XPHB} condition.

entries.name: Toughness
entries: Your {@variantrule Hit Points|XPHB|Hit Point} maximum increases by 1, and it increases by 1 again whenever you gain a level.

entries.name: Dwarven Lineage
entries: You part of a lineage that grants you special traits. Choose a lineage from the Dwarven Lineages table. You gain the level 1 benefit of that lineage. When you reach levels 3 and 5, your gain additional benefits.

If you learn a spell through your lineage the following rules apply: You always have that spell prepared. You can cast it once without a spell slot, and you regain the ability to cast it in that way when you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}. You can also cast the spell using any spell slots you have of the appropriate level. Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma is your spellcasting ability for the spells you cast with this trait (choose the ability when you select the lineage).

entries.name: Dwarven Lineages
rows: You have {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to Cold damage.

When you fail a Saving Throw, you can call upon the Great Dwarf. Roll a {@dice d20}. If the roll is equal to or less than your level, you instead succeed on the Saving Throw. Once you succeed on a Saving Throw in this way, you can't use this feature again until you finish a {@variantrule Long Rest|XPHB}.
You have {@variantrule Resistance|XPHB} to Fire damage.
You learn the {@spell Hellish Rebuke|XPHB} spell. When you cast it without a spell slot, you cast it at a level equal to your Proficiency Bonus.

When you fail an Attack Roll, you can call upon the Great Destroyer. Roll a {@dice d20}. If the roll is equal to or less than your level, you instead succeed on the Atttack Roll. Once you succeed on an Atttack Roll in this way, you can't use this feature again until you finish a {@variantrule Short Rest|XPHB}.

entries: One of the oldest races on Tyria, the Dwarves are humanoids that are stocky, short, and are almost always bearded. The dwarves live in the Shiverpeak Mountains - specifically the Northern Shiverpeaks and the Southern Shiverpeaks.

In recent years, the dwarves have been plunged into a civil war between the older Deldrimor dwarves and the recent Stone Summit faction. This war has caused unrest throughout the Shiverpeaks and held up most travel between Ascalon and the other human nations.

The exact origins of the dwarven race is unknown, but their legends claim that the Dwarves were forged by their god, the Great Dwarf, on Anvil Rock in the Northern Shiverpeaks. Over time the dwarves laid claim to the Shiverpeak Mountains, forming the Deldrimor nation. Though they traded with the human nations and were on generally good terms, the dwarves largely kept to themselves.

Sometime years prior to 1072 AE, Dagnar Stonepate, cousin to the ruling king, Jalis Ironhammer, created a splinter faction that opposed the Deldrimor. The Stone Summit was a group of xenophobic radicals who believed that it was the Dwarves' right to rule over all other races. Inevitably the friction between the two groups erupted into a civil war.
The Dwarves are a mining and smithing centric society with a monarchy.

Most of the Dwarves follow and worship the Great Dwarf as their creator and god. Outside of their creation legend, it is said that the Great Dwarf taught the Dwarves the fineness of crafting before he departed to the Great Forge, where all Dwarves go when they die. There are also dwarves who worship, or at least revere, Dwayna, Grenth, or the Great Destroyer. The dwarves who worship or revere Dwayna and Grenth only seem to appear during Wintersday. Many within the Stone Summit worship the Great Destroyer.
There is also a group of Dwarves within Deldrimor known as the Brotherhood of the Dragon who serve the oracle dragon Glint.

entries.name: Deldrimor
entries: Deldrimor is a kingdom of Dwarves residing in the Shiverpeak Mountains that are ruled by a king. They are very friendly towards other races and passage through the harsh mountain range is welcome to anyone who is a friend of Deldrimor or an enemy to the Stone Summit. They hold a capital in Thunderhead Keep.

entries.name: Stone Summit
entries: The Stone Summit is a xenophobic faction which has recently broken away from the Deldrimor dwarves while being led by Dagnar Stonepate. They have had some recent successes in seizing power and resources from the Deldrimor Dwarves, most notably in claiming the vast mine of Sorrow's Furnace, which became their main base
